title: Gradual eigenvector ergodization in coupled Ginibre matrices
abstract: Non-Hermitian random matrices provide a useful framework for understanding universal characteristics of dissipative quantum chaotic systems with loss or gain. We consider a model of two such system represented by two independent $N\times N$ complex Ginibre matrices interacting via a deterministic matrix $c{\bf 1}_N$, where $c$ is the complex coupling parameter whose magnitude $|c|$ controls the interaction strength. We characterize quantitatively how the eigenvectors of the whole system, initially localized in one of the individual subsystems for $|c|=0$, eventually spread over the full system with growing interaction strength. The resulting asymptotic formula describing such spread in the limit $N\to \infty$ is very explicit and provides a full picture of the gradual ergodization of eigenvectors as a function of the coupling parameter $|c|$ in the whole transition regime. As a by-product of our method we also compute the mean eigenvalue density for our model at the origin of the spectral bulk $z=0$ in the fully ergodic regime, when the coupling is scaled with the matrix size as $c=\sqrt{N}\tilde{c}$. We find that as $N\to \infty$ the limiting density at the origin vanishes beyond the critical value $|\tilde{c}|=1$, signalling of a split of the density support in the complex plane into two disjoint domains.
summary: This paper studies how the interaction between two separate non-Hermitian quantum systems leads to the spreading of eigenvectors across the combined system. By using a model of two coupled Ginibre matrices, the authors provide an explicit mathematical description of the transition from a state where subsystems retain their identity to a fully ergodic state. They also show that at high coupling strengths, the eigenvalue distribution can split into two separate regions in the complex plane. This provides fundamental insights into the loss of subsystem identity in dissipative quantum chaos.
main_problem: The paper investigates the gradual ergodization of eigenvectors and the splitting of eigenvalue density support in a system of two coupled non-Hermitian random matrices.
main_result: The authors derive an explicit formula for the joint probability density of eigenvalues and eigenvectors at the spectral origin and identify a critical coupling strength where the spectral support splits into two disjoint domains.
method: The study employs a non-perturbative approach using Grassmann (anticommuting) variables, the Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation, and the Laplace method for asymptotic analysis in the large N limit.
model_or_system: A 2N x 2N non-Hermitian random matrix model consisting of two independent N x N complex Ginibre matrices coupled via a deterministic matrix c*1_N, representing interacting dissipative quantum chaotic systems.
key_observables: Squared norms of eigenvector components (p1, p2), joint probability density of eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and mean eigenvalue density.
important_parameters: Coupling strength |c|, matrix size N, and the scaling limit c = sqrt(N)*c_tilde.
main_assumptions: The random matrix elements are assumed to be independent and normally distributed; the analysis focuses on the spectral origin (z=0) for mathematical tractability.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the model and the problem of eigenvector ergodization, presents a theorem for the joint probability density, provides a corollary for eigenvector norms, and concludes with a detailed mathematical appendix proving the asymptotic behavior of the integrals using the Laplace method.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers studying open quantum systems and many-body dynamics, as it provides a quantitative framework for understanding how interaction strength drives the transition from localized to ergodic states in dissipative chaotic systems like coupled SYK models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIGURE 1. The density P∞(p) plotted for the values of coupling parameter, from left to right, |c|2 = 0.125 (blue), |c|2 = 0.25 (yellow), |c|2 = 0.5 (green), |c|2 = 1 (red), |c|2 = 2 (navy blue), |c|2 = 4 (brown) and |c|2 = 8 (dark blue).
